---
title: "Virtual Context ABI"
chapter_id: "virtual-context-abi"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "vcm_public"
  - "context_engineer"
  - "verification_bandwidth"
  - "viea"
  - "vcm_editable"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `vcm_editable` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped, and support remains argument until resolver, context compiler, adequacy classifier, or VCM conformance tests exist."
  - "Only protocol schema/fixture validation is implemented; the fixture now records lifecycle state, mount scope, resolver policy, authority ceiling, adequacy requirement, consumer policy, replay boundary, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims, but no VCM resolver or context compiler tests have been run for this chapter."
  - "Lean coverage is limited to finite context-reference and lookup records; real resolver, adequacy classifier, and context compiler behavior remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `virtual-context-abi` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `vcm_public`, `context_engineer`, `verification_bandwidth`, `viea`, `vcm_editable`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `vcm_public`; supporting: `context_engineer`, `verification_bandwidth`, `viea`; connector/recovery: `vcm_editable`, `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `vcm_public`, `context_engineer`, `verification_bandwidth`, `viea`, `vcm_editable`, `moecot`; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `vcm_public`, `context_engineer`, `verification_bandwidth`, `viea`; connector/source-note only: `vcm_editable`, `moecot` |
| Test state | Context ABI fixture validation now includes lifecycle state, mount scope, resolver policy, authority ceiling, adequacy requirement, consumer policy, replay boundary, source refs, support-state effect, and non-claims; `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` finite-record proofs are implemented; resolver and compiler tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats VCM as a control-plane interface, not as a completed memory product. The repo validates a context ABI record shape and implements narrow finite-record Lean predicates for snapshot-bound reference resolution and mandatory-miss fault behavior. It does not yet contain a resolver, planner-guided context compiler, snapshot service, adequacy classifier, or conformance run.

It follows cognitive compilation because semantic atoms need source context, but they should receive a governed materialization rather than an accidental slice of transcript or retrieval output.

The ABI is the context layer's syscall surface. A planner should ask for context through stable addresses, mounts, representation needs, authority labels, and adequacy targets, not by smuggling retrieval instructions into prose.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Cognitive compilation needs context, but context should not arrive as an accidental transcript dump. The Virtual Context ABI defines the interface between durable memory and the working context a model is allowed to see: what may be mounted, which version is being used, how long the materialization lasts, and what the consumer may infer from it.

Memory access is a contract. Retrieved text, private notes, old plans, source packets, and summaries are not interchangeable just because they fit in a prompt. The ABI gives context a boundary so later reasoning can be replayed, faulted, or refused instead of reconstructed from vibes.

The aim is to make memory useful without letting every remembered thing become equally authorized context.

A good context interface makes refusal as explainable as retrieval. The missing packet, stale snapshot, or forbidden source becomes part of the answer instead of a hidden weakness.

The ABI should make context failure explicit before reasoning depends on it. A broken mount should fail loudly before it shapes action, memory, or authority.
:::

## Problem

Long-horizon agents need a stable interface between durable memory and model-visible working context.

The planning chapters define what work needs context. VCM defines how that context is addressed, compiled, represented, admitted, checked, faulted, and audited. The central distinction is durable memory versus model-visible working context: the model should receive a task-relative materialization, not a raw dump of everything the system knows.

VCM is therefore an ABI: a stable contract between planners, memory stores, context compilers, verifiers, execution adapters, and auditors. It must expose addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, adequacy states, authority labels, and typed faults.

The ABI also needs lease semantics. A materialization is valid for a task, snapshot, authority state, representation contract, and time window. It should not become ambient memory for later plans merely because it is already in the transcript.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Long context windows and ordinary retrieval do not define addressability, evidence, authority, adequacy, or fault behavior.

Long context is capacity, not governance. A retrieved passage may be stale, revoked, unauthenticated, infeasible to use, unsafe for the current worker, or inadequate for the claim being checked. A bigger window can also hide conflicts by making the model generate across text it cannot jointly verify.

Ordinary retrieval usually answers "what looks relevant?" VCM must also answer "which version, under which mount, for which task, under which authority, with which representation losses, and what happens if the context is missing, conflicting, unsafe, unknown, or unsatisfiable?"

Best-effort retrieval can be actively harmful when the task requires mandatory context. If the required context is missing, stale, unsafe, or unresolved, the correct output is a typed miss or escalation, not a plausible substitute.

## Core Claim

[virtual-context-abi.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Virtual Context Memory should expose a Virtual Context ABI with stable addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, fault classes, representation contracts, and lifecycle operations.

The claim remains at `argument` support. VCM public v1 supplies the ABI, source binding, adequacy/admission, typed non-success, and audit vocabulary. Context Engineer supplies the context-supply-chain pressure. Verification Bandwidth explains why admitted context may still be inadequate. VIEA connects context materializations to intent and evidence ledgers. The editable VCM and MoECOT sources are available for future refinement, not local implementation proof.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C maps this VCM ABI claim to all assigned source notes. Four local raw-cache mappings are now passage-reviewed. The mappings support addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, adequacy states, and typed faults as a control interface, not a deployed resolver, adequacy classifier, context compiler, or model-facing VCM result. `vcm_editable` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped until durable raw text, code, logs, conformance artifacts, benchmark records, or external corroboration are imported or inspected.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `vcm_public` | Passage-reviewed: compiled working context over a versioned semantic address space, semantic object/version/representation/validation/packet/materialization identities, source/authority binding, role-indexed obligations, adequacy/admission separation, protected compilation, semantic faults, snapshot/revocation/invalidation coherence, signed protocol boundaries, conformance coverage, and core record fields. | No local resolver, context compiler, VCM-Bench run, frozen-model result, or end-to-end superiority result exists here. |
| `context_engineer` | Passage-reviewed: context supply chains with a governor between planner and worker, layered memory, briefer agents, structured mission briefs, clearance levels, context shards, memory masks, allowed tools, Digital SCIF lifecycle, zeroization, sanitized commits, and information-flow controls. | Reported hallucination, leak, cost, and latency benchmark claims are source-reported only; no harness, raw runs, leak result, latency result, or security result is reproduced here. |
| `verification_bandwidth` | Passage-reviewed: context admission must stay separate from verification adequacy because long context, effective verification workspace, semantic-unit comparison, pairwise grinding, attention budget, transitive decay, summary loss, and contradiction-rate testing constrain verification. | The proposed contradiction-rate or adequacy tests have not been run or mechanized here. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: context materializations feed the intent-to-execution spine through structured command contracts, artifact graphs, specialist routing, context allocation, permission envelopes, claim/verification ledgers, residuals, runtime adapters, feedback, tools, benchmarks, and active-context selection. | No deployed context handoff, runtime adapter, verified VIEA execution trace, or reproduced benchmark exists in this repo. |
| `vcm_editable` | Connector/source-note mapped: refines ABI terminology around stable addresses, mounts, snapshots, materializations, representation graphs, certificates, protected compilation, authority non-escalation, semantic paging, transactions, and benchmark agenda. | Connector-readable refinement only; no local VCM-Bench or model-facing result is recorded. |
| `moecot` | Connector/source-note mapped: runtime-reference context for governed orchestration with specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, replay, handoff, readiness gates, promotion blockers, and residual tracking. | Runtime and benchmark artifacts have not been imported, inspected, or reproduced. |

## Mechanism

After semantic IR defines the atoms that need evidence, VCM defines how context is addressed and materialized for those atoms. VCM public v1 supplies semantic objects, immutable versions, representations, validations, materializations, source/authority binding, adequacy/admission separation, and typed non-success states. Context Engineer frames context as a need-to-know supply chain. Verification Bandwidth explains why admitted context can still be inadequate for a claim. VIEA ties materializations back to artifacts and ledgers, while editable VCM and MoECOT provide refinement and runtime-reference pressure without becoming local resolver evidence.

A Context ABI Record is the contract that keeps those concerns from collapsing into a raw retrieval dump.

The negative claim is explicit: the ABI is a record-level contract, not evidence that context resolution works under load. VCM public v1 justifies a record-level ABI and fault vocabulary; Context Engineer justifies the supply-chain and compartmentalization pressure; Verification Bandwidth justifies treating admitted context as potentially verification-inadequate; VIEA justifies the handoff into specialists, permission envelopes, ledgers, and runtime targets. The repository still lacks evidence for semantic-address resolution, adequacy classification, context-packet compilation under load, VCM-Bench reproduction, and validation of source-reported hallucination, leak, cost, latency, or runtime claims.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Plan context request"] --> B["Semantic address"]
  B --> C["Version + mount"]
  C --> D["Snapshot"]
  D --> E["Representation contract"]
  E --> F{"Admission allowed?"}
  F -- "yes" --> G{"Adequate for target?"}
  F -- "no" --> H["Typed fault"]
  G -- "yes" --> I["Context packet / materialization"]
  G -- "no" --> H
  I --> J["Audit + evidence refs"]
  H --> J
```

**Diagram reading note:** The ABI resolves a context request through address, version, mount, snapshot, and representation before deciding whether use is allowed and adequate. Admission and adequacy are separate gates, and a typed fault is a legitimate output rather than a hidden retrieval failure.

The ABI separates durable memory from active context and compiles task-relative packets through stable addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, authority labels, admission states, adequacy states, and typed faults. Adequacy outcomes such as adequate, missing, conflicting, unsafe, unknown, and unsatisfiable are first-class outputs, not comments appended after a best-effort retrieval.

Admission and adequacy are separate. A packet can be admitted for drafting but inadequate for verification. A source can be known but not authorized. A representation can be useful for planning but too lossy for citation or proof. These distinctions need states, not vibes.

Typed non-success states are part of the design: unknown, deferred, infeasible, unsafe, ask, abstain, deny, retry, and residual. A VCM layer that always returns "best effort context" is not a governed context layer.

The ABI should separate request validity, resolution validity, materialization validity, and adequacy validity. A request can be well formed but unresolved. A reference can resolve but be unauthorized. A materialization can be authorized but inadequate for verification. Each state should be visible before the model uses the packet.

## Interfaces

Context interoperability is carried by the Context ABI Record.

- Planning requests context.
- VCM compiles packets.
- Execution receives authority-appropriate context.

Minimum fields:

- `request_id`
- `task_id`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `request_validity`
- `semantic_address`
- `version`
- `mount`
- `mount_scope`
- `snapshot_id`
- `resolution_validity`
- `resolver_policy`
- `representation_contract`
- `authority_labels`
- `authority_ceiling`
- `materialization_validity`
- `admission_state`
- `adequacy_state`
- `adequacy_requirement`
- `fault_state`
- `materialization_ref`
- `consumer_policy`
- `support_boundary`
- `lease_expiry`
- `replay_boundary`
- `source_refs`
- `residuals`
- `audit_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

Planning requests context by task and semantic address. VCM resolves versions and mounts, compiles a representation, and emits either a materialization or a typed fault. Execution receives only context whose authority labels and representation contract are compatible with the route.

The interface should produce context receipts. A receipt says what was requested, what was resolved, what was materialized, which authority labels apply, what the representation can support, when the lease expires, and which fault or residual remains. That receipt is what lets audits replay context use.

The context-reference schema distinguishes lifecycle state, request validity, resolution validity, resolver policy, mount scope, authority ceiling, materialization validity, adequacy requirement, consumer policy, support boundary, lease expiry, replay boundary, source refs, support-state effect, residuals, and non-claims. These fields keep a well-formed request, a resolved address, an authorized materialization, and a verification-adequate packet from collapsing into one "context available" state.

## Invariants

- Addresses and versions are stable.
- Context adequacy is distinct from admission.
- Faults are typed and visible.

Address stability is what makes later verification possible: a claim should not depend on "whatever retrieval returned at the time." Snapshot validity is what makes context replay possible. Typed faults are what prevent missing context from being laundered into confident output.

Non-widening materialization keeps compiled context from becoming ambient permission. A context packet cannot broaden authority, source scope, task scope, or permitted use relative to its request and mount policy. If downstream work needs more, it asks again.

Lease expiry and permitted-use constraints travel with derived views, summaries, citations, and replay packets.

Replay packets also preserve mount faults so later auditors can distinguish missing context from accepted context.

## Failure modes

- Flat transcript memory.
- Stale context.
- Silent unsafe fit.

Flat transcript memory loses addresses, versions, and authority labels. Stale context makes a correct old answer look current. Silent unsafe fit is worse than denial because it feeds a worker context it should not see or use. Each should produce a typed fault or residual before the packet is used.

Context lease drift is the ABI failure. A packet created for drafting or review is later reused for execution, citation, or self-improvement after its snapshot, authority, or adequacy assumptions no longer hold. The ABI should force a fresh request or residual.

Fault compression weakens the same boundary when several typed context faults are summarized into one vague uncertainty note.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A context ABI table covering address, version, mount, snapshot, representation, adequacy states, and fault operations.

The context ABI begins as a `context_abi_record` schema and fixture. It validates the shape of lifecycle state, request validity, address, version, mount, mount scope, snapshot, resolution validity, resolver policy, representation, authority, authority ceiling, materialization validity, admission, adequacy, adequacy requirement, fault, materialization, consumer policy, support boundary, lease expiry, replay boundary, source refs, residual, audit, support-state effect, and non-claim fields. The Lean work is confined to the ABI lookup boundary: finite context-reference records, snapshot bindings, and lookup records; a resolved reference must have a matching address, version, and snapshot binding; and a mandatory miss must produce a typed fault rather than a best-effort materialization.

The record is an executable shape constraint. Real semantic-address resolver correctness, materialization adequacy, and deployed context-service fault coverage remain outside the Lean predicates.

A resolver fixture set would make the ABI's refusal paths visible: one valid request that resolves to a leased materialization, one mandatory miss that emits a typed fault, one resolved packet rejected for inadequate verification support, one packet denied by mount policy, and one lease-expired reuse attempt blocked. The set would test ABI semantics while leaving deployed VCM service behavior unclaimed.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The Virtual Context ABI reaches product form as the memory operating interface for long-horizon AI work. It is not a larger retrieval system. It is the stable syscall layer that lets planners, compilers, workers, verifiers, and auditors request context by address, version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority label, adequacy target, and lease boundary.

With a real context ABI, a model never receives anonymous "relevant context." It receives a materialization receipt. The receipt says what was requested, what resolved, what representation was built, which authority labels apply, which uses are permitted, when the lease expires, whether the packet is adequate for the target claim, and which fault or residual remains. If any of those states are missing, the system asks, abstains, retries, escalates, or records a typed fault rather than hallucinating a usable memory view.

A memory ABI needs:

- A resolver for semantic addresses, immutable versions, mounts, snapshots, and representation contracts.
- A context compiler that emits leased materializations with authority labels, support boundaries, consumer policies, audit refs, and non-claims.
- Typed fault behavior for missing, stale, conflicting, revoked, unauthorized, infeasible, unsafe, unknown, or inadequate context.
- Admission/adequacy separation, so drafting-acceptable packets cannot silently become verification evidence.
- Replay tools that reconstruct the exact context view used by a plan, job, claim, artifact, or release.

This context ABI remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. VCM interface support remains `argument` until resolver behavior, context-compiler traces, adequacy labels, conformance tests, and runtime audit records show what the model saw, why it was allowed to see it, what it was allowed to mean, and whether it was adequate for the work.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Context ABI fixture validation | Check that the context ABI fixture includes lifecycle state, request, address, version, mount scope, snapshot, resolver policy, representation contract, authority ceiling, admission, adequacy requirement, fault, materialization, consumer policy, replay boundary, source refs, support-state effect, and audit fields. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Snapshot-bound reference predicate | Check that a resolved finite context-reference record has a matching address, version, and snapshot binding. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI`; builds locally |
| Admission vs adequacy test | Check that admitted context can still be labeled inadequate for a target claim. | planned; not run |
| Conflict adequacy classification test | Check that conflicting context emits a typed adequacy or fault state. | planned; not run |
| Mandatory-miss fault predicate | Check that mandatory misses produce typed faults instead of best-effort packets. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI`; builds locally |
| Resolver conformance test | Check that a deployed resolver rejects invalid addresses, versions, mounts, and snapshots before materialization. | planned; not run |

The fixture-shape check and finite-record proof predicates are implemented. Resolver behavior, adequacy classification, and service-level fault emission require a context compiler or deterministic test harness.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:vcm.abi.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` | A context reference resolves only when the requested address and version are valid for the snapshot. | implemented |
| `lean:vcm.abi.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` | A mandatory context miss produces a typed fault rather than a best-effort packet. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented only over finite Lean context-reference and lookup records. `AsiStackProofs.VirtualContextABI` checks that a resolved reference has a matching address, version, and snapshot binding, and that a mandatory miss emits a typed fault with no best-effort materialization. The proof does not implement a VCM resolver, adequacy classifier, snapshot service, or context compiler.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `vcm_public` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1 | memory_context | Public VCM release. Governed protocol for compiled working context. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `context_engineer` | Context Engineer / Manhattan Protocol | memory_context_lineage | Context as supply chain; compartmentalized information logistics. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `verification_bandwidth` | Verification Bandwidth in Bounded Contexts | context_verification_theory | Generation vs verification; long context is not automatically long-range reasoning. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache passage-reviewed |
| `vcm_editable` | Virtual_Context_Memory_v1.0_Editable | memory_context | Editable version with evidence-carrying planner-guided context compiler framing. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The VCM source set anchors the ABI and context-supply-chain vocabulary. Four mappings have passage review, but the core claim stays at `argument` because connector mappings remain unreviewed and resolver behavior, adequacy classification, context compilation, VCM conformance artifacts, model-facing results, and benchmark claims remain unpromoted.

## Summary

The Virtual Context ABI is the memory/context contract. It keeps durable memory separate from working context while making addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation contracts, authority, adequacy, faults, materializations, and audits explicit.

The rule is simple: context that cannot be addressed, versioned, authorized, represented, and faulted should not be silently treated as usable memory. The materialized packet then needs internal rules for what each page, cell, and summary must carry so compressed context does not impersonate evidence.

Context access should be replayable. If a future auditor cannot reconstruct why the model saw a packet, what it was allowed to mean, and when its authority expired, the ABI did not do its job. This is one of the central differences between ordinary context engineering and governed context. The goal is not simply to fit more material into a prompt or retrieve more relevant snippets. The goal is to make context an authorized, versioned, lossy-or-lossless, evidence-bounded object whose misuse can be detected. Only then can memory support planning, verification, tool use, and self-improvement without becoming an invisible source of overconfidence.

## Handoff

The ABI names memory handles and access rules, but the materialized context still has to carry provenance, compression loss, authority, and permitted uses at a smaller unit of work. Semantic Pages, Context Cells, and Certificates gives those packets internal structure. It turns retrieved or compressed material into inspectable cells whose evidence limits can travel with the representation instead of disappearing into prompt text.
